docs.title: 👋 About Me
docs.text:
Hi, I'm Anshuman Singh (Vritra0).

I am an aspiring SOC Analyst passionate about Detection Engineering, Threat Hunting, Malware Analysis, and Security Operations.

This website showcases my hands-on investigations, cybersecurity labs, detection engineering projects, and technical writeups as I continue building practical blue team skills.

My objective is to contribute to real-world security operations by detecting threats, analyzing attacks, and creating effective detection strategies.

docs.title: SOC002
docs.text:
Batch Downloader — LetsDefend Challenge Walkthrough

Note: This is my first time writing a cybersecurity blog. If you notice any mistakes or have suggestions for improvement, I’d love to hear your feedback!

In this write-up, we’ll solve the Batch Downloader challenge from LetsDefend. This challenge focuses on analyzing a malicious Windows batch script and answering questions based on its behavior.

Let’s get started!

Challenge Description The challenge description states:

“This malicious batch file downloads and executes files related to Laplas Clipper malware.”

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Description of the challenge Since I wasn’t familiar with Laplas Clipper malware, I did a quick search before diving into the analysis. Understanding the malware family often provides useful context when examining malicious scripts.

Laplas Clipper Step 1: Access the Lab After connecting to the LetsDefend lab environment, you’ll receive a ZIP archive.

Open the ZIP file. Extract its contents. Locate the original challenge file. Open the batch file using Notepad++ . Once opened, you’ll see the contents of the malicious batch script. Press enter or click to view image in full size

Challenge Questions & Solutions Question 1 What command is used to prevent the command echoing in the console?

Answer:

@echo off This command prevents each command from being displayed in the command prompt while the script executes.

Question 2 Which tool is used to download a file from a specified URL in the script?

Answer:

bitsadmin bitsadmin is a Windows command-line utility that can download files using the Background Intelligent Transfer Service (BITS). Although originally designed for legitimate file transfers, it is frequently abused by malware.

Question 3 What is the priority set for the download operation in the script?

Write on Medium Answer:

FOREGROUND The script configures the BITS download job to use the FOREGROUND priority.

Question 4 Which command is used to start localization of environment changes in the script?

Answer:

setlocal setlocal ensures that any environment variable changes made by the script remain local and do not affect the system after the script finishes.

Question 5 Which IP address is used by the malicious code?

Answer:

193.169.255.78 This IP address is used by the batch script to retrieve the malicious payload.

Question 6 What is the name of the subroutine called to extract the contents of the ZIP file?

Answer:

UnZipFile The script invokes the UnZipFile subroutine to extract the downloaded archive.

Question 7 Which command attempts to start the executable extracted from the ZIP file?

Answer:

start "" "FW-APGKSDTPX4HOAUJJMBVDNXPOHZ.PDF.exe" This command launches the extracted executable, which is the final stage of the infection chain.

Question 8 Which scripting language is used to extract the contents of the ZIP file?

Answer:

VBScript The batch script leverages VBScript to automate the extraction of the ZIP archive.

Conclusion This challenge provides a great introduction to analyzing malicious Windows batch scripts. Even though the script is relatively simple, it demonstrates several techniques commonly used by malware, including:

Downloading payloads using bitsadmin Extracting ZIP archives with VBScript Executing downloaded files Using batch scripting features like setlocal and @echo off Understanding these techniques is an essential skill for malware analysts and SOC analysts, as similar patterns appear frequently in real-world incidents.

Thank you for reading! If you found this walkthrough helpful, feel free to leave feedback or share your own approach to solving the challenge.
